coredata.dc:title: Chemiluminescence of graphene quantum dots and its application to the determination of uric acid
coredata.prism:copyright: Copyright © 2014 Elsevier B.V. All rights reserved.
coredata.dc:creator: Amjadi, Mohammad
Manzoori, Jamshid L.
Hallaj, Tooba
coredata.dc:description: We report on the chemiluminescence (CL) of graphene quantum dots (GQDs) induced by direct chemical oxidation. GQDs were prepared by a simple carbonization method and characterized by X-ray diffraction, Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy, transmission electron microscopy and Raman spectroscopy. It was found that Ce(IV) could oxidize GQDs to produce a relatively intense CL emission. The mechanism of CL generation was investigated based on the fluorescence and CL emission spectra. It was attributed to the radiative recombination of oxidant-injected holes and thermally excited electrons in the GQDs. In order to show the analytical application potential of GQDs-Ce(IV) CL system, it was applied to the determination of uric acid. Under the optimized conditions, the proposed CL system exhibited excellent analytical performance for determination of uric acid in the range of 1.0×10−6 M–5.0×10−4 M with a limit of detection of 5.0×10−7 M. The method was applied to the determination of uric acid in human plasma and urine samples, with satisfactory results.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Graphene quantum dots
Chemiluminescence
Uric acid
Nanomaterial

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Analytical application of the CL system

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: TEM image of the prepared GQDs. (b) XRD pattern for the GQDs. (c) FT-IR spectrum of GQDs. (d) Raman spectrum of GQDs.

(a) UV–vis absorption spectrum of GQDs (with 30-fold dilution). The inset is UV–vis absorption spectrum of GQDs with 10-fold dilution. (b) Fluorescence spectra for GQDs excited at 340–420nm wavelength range, with increments of 20nm.

Effect of different concentrations of oxidants on the CL intensity of GQDs (200µL). All oxidants were in basic medium (NaOH, 0.1M) except KMnO4 (2) that was in acidic medium (H2SO4, 0.02M) and Ce(IV), which prepared in sulfuric acid medium (0.2M).
Kinetic curve for GQDs-Ce(IV) CL system. Conditions: GQDs, 500µL, Ce(IV) (2×10−3 M) and H2SO4 (0.04M).

(a) CL spectrum of GQDs-Ce(IV) system, obtained with continuous flow of reagents: Ce(IV) (4×10−3 M) and H2SO4 (0.08M) in one line and GQD solution in other line; (b) fluorescence spectrum of GQDs, at λ ex=400nm.

Optimization of the CL reaction conditions: (a) Effect of Ce(IV) concentration. Conditions: GQD (400µL) and H2SO4 (0.04M); (b) effect of H2SO4 concentration. Conditions: Ce(IV), 2×10−3 M; other conditions are as in a (c) effect of GQD solution volume, Conditions: H2SO4 (0.04M); other conditions are as in b.
Comparison of the developed CL method for the determination uric acid with some previously published CL methods.
Results for the determination of uric acid in plasma and urine samples.

head.ce:title: Chemiluminescence of graphene quantum dots and its application to the determination of uric acid

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Analytical Chemistry, Faculty of Chemistry, University of Tabriz, Tabriz 5166616471, Iran

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: We report on the chemiluminescence (CL) of graphene quantum dots (GQDs) induced by direct chemical oxidation. GQDs were prepared by a simple carbonization method and characterized by X-ray diffraction, Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy, transmission electron microscopy and Raman spectroscopy. It was found that Ce(IV) could oxidize GQDs to produce a relatively intense CL emission. The mechanism of CL generation was investigated based on the fluorescence and CL emission spectra. It was attributed to the radiative recombination of oxidant-injected holes and thermally excited electrons in the GQDs. In order to show the analytical application potential of GQDs-Ce(IV) CL system, it was applied to the determination of uric acid. Under the optimized conditions, the proposed CL system exhibited excellent analytical performance for determination of uric acid in the range of 1.0×10−6 M–5.0×10−4 M with a limit of detection of 5.0×10−7 M. The method was applied to the determination of uric acid in human plasma and urine samples, with satisfactory results.

ce:section.ce:para: Graphene, a single layer of carbon atoms in a honeycomb structure, have gained considerable interest because of their fascinating physical and chemical properties [1,2]. A new member of graphene family, which has been discovered very recently, is graphene quantum dots (GQDs). They are graphene sheets smaller than 100nm that exhibit numerous unique physicochemical properties due to the pronounced quantum confinement and edge effects. For example, unlike graphene that does not exhibit photoluminescence (PL) because of its zero-bandgap, GQDs prepared via different approaches can emit intense PL with different colors. Compared to the common carbon-based photoluminescent nanomaterials (carbon dots), GQDs have some excellent characteristics, such as higher surface areas, larger diameters and better surface grafting properties. Also, unlike carbon dots which usually contain amorphous carbon, GQDs clearly possess graphene lattices inside the dots regardless of the dot size [3]. Moreover, compared to semiconductor quantum dots, GQDs show considerably lower toxicity, excellent solubility, chemical inertia, stable PL, high electrical and thermal conductivity [3–5]. As a result of these superior properties, GQDs have attracted the growing attention in many areas and applications such as bioimaging [6,7], photovoltaic devices [8,9], electrochemical catalysis and biosensors [10]. However, there are only a few reports on the fluorescence [11–14] and electrochemiluminescence studies of GQDs [15,16]. To the best of our knowledge, the chemiluminescence (CL) properties of GQDs have not yet been studied, though the CL of carbon dots has been reported before [17–22].

CL, light emission induced by chemical reactions, is an attractive analytical method due to several advantages such as high sensitivity, simplicity of instrumentation, low detection limit, large calibration range and short analysis time. Recently, the CL studies have been extended from traditional molecular systems to the nanoparticle-based systems. In these systems, nanoparticles can participate as a catalyst, reductant, luminophore, and energy acceptor [23–26]. Among nanoparticles, some QDs such as CdSe/CdS [27], CdTe [28–31] and CdS [32] have been applied as the CL emitters. However, the inherent toxicity of Cd-based semiconductors may raise serious health and environmental problems [33,34]. Therefore, it is necessary to find a safer alternative to semiconductor QDs with good CL activities.

Uric acid is the end product of purine catabolism in humans. At physiological pH, uric acid is mostly ionized and therefore is present mainly as the urate ion. When serum urate level exceeds the solubility limit, sodium urate crystallizes in soft tissues and joints and causes an inflammatory reaction, gouty arthritis. Other disorders of purine catabolism include Lesch–Nyhan syndrome, von Gierke׳s disease, and hypouricemias [35]. Therefore, monitoring of uric acid in human plasma and urine is of great importance in the physiological investigations and disease diagnosis.

In the present work, direct CL of GQDs induced by some common oxidants was investigated. The mechanism of GQD CL system was proposed based on the fluorescence and CL emission spectra. Moreover, based on the diminishing effect of uric acid on the GQDs-Ce(IV) CL system, a simple and sensitive method was proposed for the determination of trace amount of uric acid in human plasma and urine samples.

The CL signals were monitored by LUMAT LB 9507 chemiluminometer (Berthold; www.berthold.com). CL spectrum was recorded by RF-540 spectrofluorimeter (Shimadzu, Japan) using flow mode with the excitation light source being turned off. The fluorescence spectra were also recorded by the same instrument under normal conditions. UV–vis spectra were recorded on a Cary-100 Spectrophotometer (Varian; www.varianinc.com). The size and shape of GQDs were characterized by transmission electron microscopy (TEM, Leo 906, Zeiss, Germany). The Fourier transform infrared (FTIR) spectrum was obtained on a FT-IR spectrophotometer (Tensor 27, Bruker). X-ray powder diffraction (XRD) pattern was measured by a Siemens D 500 instrument (Germany). Raman spectrum was recorded using an Almega Thermo Nicolet Dispersive Raman Spectrometer (USA) with a 532nm laser.

All reagents were of analytical reagent grade. Doubly distilled deionized water (obtained from Ghazi Serum Co. Tabriz, Iran) was used throughout the experiment. Citric acid monohydrate, Sodium hydroxide (NaOH) and Ce(SO4)2·4H2O were all purchased from Merck (Darmstadt, Germany). A stock standard solution of 1.0×10−2 M uric acid was prepared by dissolving a proper amount of uric acid (Merck) in a minimum volume of 0.1M NaOH and diluting with water. Working standard solutions were prepared by appropriate dilution of the stock solution. Ce(IV) solution (0.01M) was prepared in 0.2M H2SO4.

GQDs were synthesized by pyrolyzing citric acid according to the literature with some modifications [36]. Briefly, 2.0g of citric acid was put into a beaker and heated to 200°C by a heating mantle until the citric acid changed to an orange liquid. Then, for preparing GQDs, 100mL of 10mgmL−1 NaOH solution was added into the orange liquid dropwise with continuous, vigorous stirring. The obtained GQD solution was stable for at least one month in the refrigerator at 4°C.

CL analyses were carried out in a 3mL tube, in the batch condition. An amount of 500μL of GQD was added into the cell. Then an appropriate volume of sample or standard solution was added and the volume was reached to 800μL with distilled water. After injection of 200μL of 0.01M Ce(IV) by an automatic injector, monitoring of CL signal versus time was started automatically. Maximum CL intensity was used as the analytical signal.

Human plasma samples were obtained from Blood Transfusion Center (Tabriz, Iran). A 500µL aliquot of plasma was placed into a centrifuge tube and 2.0mL of 0.1M Ba(OH)2 and 1.8mL of 0.1M ZnSO4 were added to precipitate the proteins. Then the solution was centrifuged for 15min. The supernatant solution was transferred into a 5.0mL volumetric flask and diluted to the mark with water. An appropriate portion of this solution was analyzed according to the general procedure.
No pretreatment was necessary for urine samples. 100µL of urine was taken for analysis according to the general procedure.

In this work, a simple carbonization method was used for synthesis of GQDs. The TEM image of synthesized GQDs ( Fig. 1a) shows that they are of spherical shape and nearly mono disperse nanoparticles with size distribution in the range of 5±2nm. The XRD pattern of GQDs shows a broad (002) peak around 25θ (0.36nm), suggesting that carbonizing citric acid produces graphite structures (Fig. 1b). The FT-IR spectrum of GQDs is shown in Fig. 1c. The peaks of COO− at about 1574 and 1397cm−1 and stretching vibrations of carboxyl groups at 3121cm−1, can be observed in this spectrum. These peaks show that GQDs contain –COOH groups. Additionally, the peak at about 2927cm−1 is attributed to the stretching vibration of C–H, suggesting that the GQDs contain some incompletely carbonized citric acid. To further characterize the GQDs, their Raman spectrum was obtained. Raman spectrum of graphene is characterized by two main features (G and D peaks). The G peak corresponds to the E 2g phonon at the Brillioun zone center. The D peak is due to the breathing modes of sp2 rings and requires a defect for its activation [37,38]. As shown in Fig. 1d, the synthesized GQDs exhibit two characteristic peaks around 1370 and 1550cm−1, corresponding to the D and G band of graphene, respectively. The relatively high intensity of D band indicated the presence of defects in the GQD structure.

The prepared GQDs exhibited a blue color under ultraviolet radiation (λ=365nm). The UV–vis absorption spectrum of GQDs ( Fig. 2a) exhibits typical absorption of graphene derivatives in the UV region (200–300nm) with an intensive peak at ~210nm which was assigned to the π–π ⁎ transition of aromatic sp2 domains. The n–π⁎ transition peak at ~360nm was also observed (3). The fluorescence behavior of GQDs was investigated. The maximum emission of ~470nm was obtained with an excitation wavelength of 400nm. As shown in Fig. 2b, the prepared GQDs showed an excitation-independent PL feature. When the excitation wavelength changes from 340 to 420nm, the PL spectra are almost invariable. This could be explained by the uniformity both in the size and the surface state of those sp2 clusters contained in GQDs which was responsible for the fluorescence of GQDs. The fluorescence quantum yield of the prepared GQDs was found to be about 12% by using quinine sulfate as a standard.

Initially, we investigated the effect of various oxidants commonly applied in CL reactions (including K3Fe(CN)6, KMnO4, H2O2, Ce(IV) and NaIO4) on the GQD solution. Our studies indicated that all of these oxidants, except H2O2, are able to directly elicit a CL from GQDs. Then, the reactions between GQDs and various concentrations of these oxidants were examined. As shown in Fig. 3, the CL produced by Ce(IV) was more intensive than that of the other oxidants. Thus, Ce(IV) was selected as the best oxidant for the CL reaction. A control experiment with the reagents used for the preparation of GQDs (the mixture of citric acid and NaOH) was also carried out. Very weak CL was found for this solution, which confirms that the CL arises from GQDs.

It is interesting to compare these results with those obtained for solvothermally prepared carbon dots in our previous work [22]. This comparison reveals that the CL behavior of GQDs is different from that of carbon dots. While all of the examined oxidants were able to elicit a CL from carbon dots, we found that H2O2 cannot produce a CL from GQDs. Furthermore, the CL intensity of GQDs with all other oxidants was higher than that of carbon dots. Finally, the maximum wavelength of CL spectrum of the GQDs/oxidant systems is different from that of carbon dots/oxidant systems.
The kinetic profile of GQDs-Ce(IV) CL system ( Fig. 4) showed that the CL reaction was very fast and the CL intensity reached a maximum value within 1s after injection of the oxidant solution.

In order to identify the emitting species in the CL reaction, the CL emission spectrum of GQDs-Ce(IV) system was obtained using a spectrofluorimeter in flow mode. The obtained CL spectrum ( Fig. 5a) indicated that there was an emission band in the range of 400–600nm with a maximum at ~470nm. This CL spectrum is quite similar to the GQD fluorescence spectrum (Fig. 5b). Therefore, the CL can be attributed to the excited GQDs.

In order to confirm the reaction of GQDs with Ce(IV), the fluorescence spectrum of GQDs was recorded after mixing with this oxidant. According to the results, after addition of Ce(IV) to GQD solution, the fluorescence peak of GQDs at 470nm decreased. So it can be concluded that the chemical reaction occurred between GQDs and Ce(IV). Based on these facts, the CL reaction can be described in its simplest form as follows:
GQD+Ce(IV)→GQD⁎+Ce(III)
GQD⁎→GQD+hν (~470nm)

Obviously, the CL behavior of GQDs is similar to that of other QDs, such as CdTe [28], CDs [17], and Si NCs [39]. Consequently, the CL mechanism of the GQDs was suggested to involve the formation of excited-state GQDs (GQDs⁎) through electron-transfer (ET) annihilation.

Oxidants, such as Ce(IV), could inject holes into the GQDs. The thermally excited electrons in the high energy band annihilate with the oxidant-injected holes to produce the CL. Obviously, as the temperature rises above absolute zero, there is more energy to excite electrons into the high energy levels. The injection of holes into the GQDs by oxidants increases the holes in the GQDs and accelerates the electrons–hole annihilation, which results in energy release in the form of CL emission [17]. It should be mentioned that de-oxygenation of the solution caused little reduction in the CL intensity, which indicated that the dissolved oxygen had little effect on the CL.
In order to obtain the maximum CL intensity, the effects of several analytical variables such as the concentration of Ce(IV), H2SO4 and GQDs on the CL intensity were investigated.

Solutions with different concentration of Ce(SO4)2·4H2O were prepared over the range of 5×10−4 to 3×10−3 M and their CL intensities were measured. As seen from Fig. 6a, the CL intensity was found to increase with increasing the concentration of Ce(IV) up to 2×10−3 M. But a decrease in CL intensity was observed at higher concentrations. Thus, a concentration of 2×10−3 M was selected as the optimum Ce(IV) concentration for further studies.

The effect of sulfuric acid concentration in Ce(IV) solution on the CL signal was also studied and the results are shown in Fig. 6b. The CL signal increased by increasing the sulfuric acid concentration until 0.04M and then decreased at higher concentrations. The decrease in CL intensity at lower concentrations is probably due to hydrolysis of Ce(IV) forming cerium hydroxide. Based on these results, 0.04M sulfuric acid was used for further work.

Finally, the influence of the amount of GQD solution on the CL intensity was examined in the range of 50–500μL of prepared GQD stock solution. As shown in Fig. 6c the CL intensity was increased by increasing GQD volume up to 500µL, and then remained constant. Therefore, this amount was selected as the optimum value for consequent experiments.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Analytical application of the CL system
ce:section.ce:para: It was found that trace amount of uric acid has a diminishing effect on the CL reaction. This effect can be attributed to the competition of uric acid with GQDs for Ce(IV). The UV–vis absorption peak of uric acid at 290nm decreases with adding Ce(IV), which confirms the oxidation of uric acid by Ce(IV). Based on this phenomenon, a sensitive method was developed for the determination of uric acid. Under the optimum conditions described above, the analytical figures of merit for the determination of uric acid was obtained. The CL response was found to be linear in the concentration range of 1.0×10−6–5.0×10−4 M with a limit of detection (3s) of 5.0×10−7 M. The regression equation was ΔI=(6×108) C+22263, R 2=0.9996, where ΔI=I−I 0 is the difference between CL intensity in the presence of uric acid (I) and in its absence (I 0), and C is concentration of uric acid (M). The relative standard deviation (RSD) was obtained to be 2.4% for five determinations of 5×10−6 M uric acid. The results indicate that this CL system has good linearity, relatively high sensitivity and suitable precision. Comparison between the proposed method and some other reported CL methods for the uric acid quantification is shown in Table 1. The analytical performances of the developed method are comparable with those of other CL-based methods. However, our method is quite simpler and less expensive than most of other methods. Furthermore, GQDs-Ce(IV) is a new CL system which may have potential applications in the analysis of some other compounds.

In order to evaluate the selectivity of the proposed method, the effects of some common inorganic ions and organic compounds on the determination of 1.0×10−5 M uric acid were investigated. The tolerable concentration ratios for interferences in relative error of <5% were over 5000 for K+, Ca2+, NO3 −, Cl−, SO4 2−, PO4 3−, 2000 for Na+, glucose, sucrose, lactose, alanine, 1000 for Zn2+, 500 for glycine, 400 for l-cysteine, 100 for Mg2+, Cu2+, Fe3+, glutathione, tryptophan and 5 for ascorbic acid. As can be seen, the amounts of most potentially interfering species in plasma are below their tolerable levels or can be decreased with diluting, so there would be no interferences from these species in uric acid determination. Ascorbic acid exhibits a remarkable interference effect, however, its concentration in plasma is normally about 28 to 68μM [40], which is quite lower than the normal concentration of uric acid and will be further decreased below tolerance limit with dilution of samples in pretreatment step. These results demonstrate that the method possesses a good selectivity for the determination uric acid in biological fluids.

The present method was easily applied to the determination of uric acid in human plasma and urine samples. The previously described procedure for deproteinization in experimental section was found to be necessary, and diluting process was done for all samples to make the final concentrations of uric acid in the linear range.

Recovery tests were also performed to evaluate the accuracy of this method. For this purpose, known quantities of uric acid were added into the samples before any pretreatment, and then the samples were prepared and analyzed according the general procedure. The obtained results are summarized in Table 2. Statistical analysis of these results using Student t-test showed that there are no significant differences between added and found values.

In this work, we demonstrated that Ce(IV) as a common CL reagent could directly oxidize GQDs to give rise to a relatively strong CL emission. A mechanism for the CL system was proposed, which involves the reaction of Ce(IV) with GQDs to inject holes into them. Then, the thermally excited electrons annihilate with the Ce(IV)-injected holes to produce the excited-state GQD⁎, which acts as the final emitter in the CL system. A new analytical method for the determination of uric acid was developed based on its diminishing effect on the GQDs-Ce(IV) CL system. The method has a good linearity, high sensitivity and good repeatability and can be applied to the determination of uric acid in human plasma and urine samples.

ce:other-ref.ce:textref: M. Bacon, S.J. Bradley, T. Nann, Part. Part. Syst. Charact. 10.1002/ppsc.201300252
